title: Type Checking Tool Arguments
concept: Validating model-generated tool arguments against a schema (like JSON Schema or Pydantic) before calling.

exercises.prompt: Welcome to Parameter Validation! When an AI agent decides to use a tool, what does it actually produce?

options.text: A set of arguments (like the values to fill into the tool)
The finished result of the tool, skipping the tool
A brand new tool it invents from scratch

exercises.explain: When an agent calls a tool, it generates the arguments (inputs) to hand over. The tool then runs with those inputs. Because the model wrote those inputs, we should double-check them first!

cards.title: The Model Can Fib
cards.body: An AI might call a weather tool with city set to the number 42 instead of "Paris". If you run the tool blindly, it crashes or gives nonsense. We need a bouncer at the door.

cards.title: Type Checking Tool Arguments
cards.body: Before running a tool, you compare the model's arguments to a schema (a rulebook that says which fields exist and what type each should be). If something doesn't match, you reject it.

cards.title: Passport Control
cards.body: A schema is like an airport checkpoint. It checks your name is text, your age is a number, and no fields are missing. Only valid 'passengers' get through to the tool.

cards.title: Weather Tool Check
cards.body: Schema says: city must be text, days must be a whole number. Model sends city: "Paris", days: "three". The check fails because "three" is text, not a number. Caught before it crashes!

cards.title: "The Model Always Gets It Right"
cards.body: Nope! Models often send strings where numbers belong, misspell field names, or forget required fields. Validation is your safety net, not an insult to the model.

cards.title: Valid vs Invalid Arguments
cards.body: A schema sorts good arguments from bad ones before any tool runs.

left.text: city: "Paris", days: 3 — text and number match the schema.

right.text: city: 42, days: "three" — types are wrong, so it's rejected.

exercises.prompt: A tool schema requires 'temperature' to be a number. The model sends temperature: "hot". What should validation do?

options.text: Reject it because "hot" is text, not a number
Run the tool anyway and hope for the best
Change the schema to accept any word

exercises.explain: The schema demands a number, but "hot" is text. Good validation catches the mismatch and rejects it before the tool runs, preventing a crash or garbage output.

exercises.prompt: True or False: Tools like JSON Schema or Pydantic let you define the expected type of each tool argument and check the model's output against it.
exercises.explain: True! JSON Schema and Pydantic are exactly the rulebooks you use to declare each field's type and then validate the model's arguments against those rules.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the blank about checking tool inputs.
exercises.sentence: Before calling a tool, we compare the model's arguments to a schema to make sure each field has the correct type.
exercises.explain: A schema is the rulebook listing each field and its expected type. Comparing arguments to it is how we type-check before calling the tool.

exercises.prompt: Which validation error message was written by an AI to sound clear and helpful?

options.text: Field 'days' expected a number but received the text "three". Please provide a whole number like 3.

exercises.explain: The AI-written message names the exact field, explains what type was expected versus received, and suggests a fix — clear and actionable, unlike the vague or cryptic ones.

exercises.prompt: An agent calls a booking tool. Schema: guests = whole number (required), name = text (required). The model sends name: "Sam", guests: "two". What's the best action?

options.text: Reject the call: guests is "two" (text) but the schema requires a whole number, so it fails validation before the tool runs.
Run the booking tool immediately since name is valid.
Ignore the schema — models are always right about types.
Book zero guests to be safe.

exercises.explain: Even though name is fine, the tool can't run until ALL required fields match their types. Here guests came in as text ("two") instead of a whole number, so validation should reject it before calling the tool. The big takeaway: always type-check model-generated arguments against your schema first — it's your safety net against crashes and nonsense.
